cataloging_subject.label: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The intellectual and editorial unit published by Carl Horstmann in Archiv 57 (1877), pp. 241-316, and represented as CME00062: forty-five selected Middle English verse narratives from the Northern Homily Cycle and associated material in the Vernon Manuscript, German and Latin editorial headings and notes, and a thirty-four-line addendum drawn from a separate place in the Cana homily. It is not the complete Northern Homily Cycle, not the whole Vernon Manuscript, and not a single medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
catalog_summary.date: Original Northern Homily Cycle probably composed around 1300 or in the early fourteenth century; Vernon expanded recension copied 1381-1400, commonly c. 1390; edited selection published 1877
catalog_summary.region: Original cycle associated with northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; Vernon copy associated tentatively with the West Midlands; Horstmann's editorial dateline is Sagan (Zagan), 1876

titles.value: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon

notes: The final phrase is a statement of editorial responsibility, not medieval authorship.

titles.value: Gospel Stories from the Homily Collection of the Vernon Manuscript
notes: Stories is intentionally broad: the selection contains exempla, saints' legends, miracle narratives, and other embedded religious tales.

summaries.value: Horstmann's article extracts forty-five verse narratives from the Northern Homily Cycle and associated material in the Vernon Manuscript. The stories range across conversion, penance, saints, monastic life, Marian aid, Eucharistic miracles, judgment, forgiveness, and salvation. German editorial notes compare the opening section with John Small's edition of the earliest surviving witness and distinguish the substantially revised Harley-Cotton recension. A final addendum prints thirty-four lines from a separate point in the Cana homily.

The forty-five numbered selections include Marian and penitential exempla; three short desert-saint tales in item 6; two Marian tales in item 12; the Eustace/Placidas legend; conversion legends of Thais and Pelagia; a multi-part Corpus Christi and Eucharistic miracle sequence in item 29; Theophilus; Gregory and Trajan; Esther; and Piers the Toller. The source prints two successive items as number 15 and then continues at 17. The addendum is unnumbered.

Catalog dates and places separate the original cycle, the Vernon expanded recension and witness, Horstmann's 1876 dateline, and 1877 publication. The selected tales are not collapsed into one medieval composition, and manuscript folios are not treated as article page numbers.

extent.description: Forty-five printed numbered selections (with number 15 duplicated and 16 absent) plus one unnumbered thirty-four-line addendum. The XML contains 7,359 lines in the numbered selections and 34 in the addendum, for 7,393 L elements total, grouped in 111 LG elements. Three prose P elements belong to editorial framing, not prose-only medieval items.

content_parts.label: 1. Conversion of Mary Magdalene
content_parts.summary: An eighty-two-line conversion narrative headed De conversione Marie Magdalene.

content_parts.label: 2. A dead monk reveals his state
content_parts.summary: A ninety-line monastic otherworld exemplum.

content_parts.label: 3. Death of John the Baptist
content_parts.summary: An eighty-five-line narrative of John's death, presented in place of a tale.

content_parts.label: 4. The impoverished knight devoted to the Virgin
content_parts.summary: A 122-line Marian exemplum absent from Small's principal Edinburgh witness.

content_parts.label: 5. The pilgrim to St James
content_parts.summary: A 138-line penitential miracle concerning a pilgrim who mutilates himself.

content_parts.label: 6. Three desert-saint exempla
content_parts.summary: A 126-line group: the devil appears to St Martin; humility escapes the devil's wiles; and Amon trusts wholly in God.

content_parts.label: 7. The archbishop and the nun
content_parts.summary: A 382-line episcopal and monastic exemplum with several Small variants noted by Horstmann.

content_parts.label: 8. The bishop misjudges St John's child
content_parts.summary: A 108-line narrative concerning St John and a boy.

content_parts.label: 9. Spiritual things should not be sold
content_parts.summary: A sixty-line tale of Gehazi and Naaman against simony.

content_parts.label: 10. The knight's penance among worms
content_parts.summary: A 142-line penitential exemplum.

content_parts.label: 11. Macarius sees the devil's medicines
content_parts.summary: A one-hundred-line desert-father exemplum.

content_parts.label: 12. Two miracles of the Virgin
content_parts.summary: A 260-line group: the Virgin gives a good wife a candle, and delivers a pregnant abbess.

content_parts.label: 13. The hermit called but not chosen
content_parts.summary: A forty-six-line eremitic exemplum.

content_parts.label: 14. St Marina is defamed
content_parts.summary: A 228-line Marina legend; Horstmann cites a different version in Harley 2253 fol. 64 and an analogous Theodora tale in Vernon under legacy fol. 92.

content_parts.label: 15. A Pater Noster for St Bernard's palfrey
content_parts.summary: A fifty-two-line St Bernard exemplum.

content_parts.label: 15 [sequence 16]. The story of Placidas
content_parts.summary: A 1,060-line legend of Eustace/Placidas. Horstmann prints a second number 15 and then continues with 17; sequence 16 is supplied only for navigation.
notes: Do not silently normalize the printed heading to 16 in source-facing displays.

content_parts.label: 17. A hermit drives another to despair
content_parts.summary: A 164-line eremitic narrative of despair and judgment.

content_parts.label: 18. Stones answer St Bede
content_parts.summary: A forty-line miracle narrative associated with Bede.

content_parts.label: 19. The wife and her husband's shirt
content_parts.summary: A seventy-two-line memorial and marital exemplum.

content_parts.label: 20. Of St Martin
content_parts.summary: A thirty-six-line St Martin narrative.

content_parts.label: 21. The devil tempts sleepers during preaching
content_parts.summary: A twenty-six-line exemplum about attention to God's word.

content_parts.label: 22. The devil would have encumbered St Edmund
content_parts.summary: A sixteen-line St Edmund exemplum.

content_parts.label: 23. The founder of Clairvaux saved by a leper
content_parts.summary: A sixty-six-line charitable exemplum associated with the founder of Clairvaux.

content_parts.label: 24. The monk who desired to see the least bliss of Paradise
content_parts.summary: A ninety-eight-line vision or otherworld exemplum.

content_parts.label: 25. Carpus
content_parts.summary: A sixty-six-line Carpus exemplum.

content_parts.label: 26. The obedient servant
content_parts.summary: A twenty-six-line obedience exemplum without a descriptive printed heading.

content_parts.label: 27. Conversion of Thais
content_parts.summary: A fifty-four-line conversion legend of Thais the courtesan.

content_parts.label: 28. The king frightens his brother with minstrelsy
content_parts.summary: A fifty-four-line royal exemplum that Horstmann parallels with Barlaam and Josaphat.

content_parts.label: 29. Feast of Corpus Christi and Eucharistic miracles
content_parts.summary: A 724-line multi-part selection: two Eucharistic explanations or narratives followed by selected Septem Miracula. Horstmann omits about one hundred introductory lines before the miracles; the current Vernon structure identifies the latter passage as an extract from Robert of Brunne's Handlyng Synne.

locators.value: parts a-b fols. 196r-197r, canvases 279-281; part c fols. 197r-199r, canvases 281-285

notes: The printed heading says seven miracles, while the XML exposes labeled subitems A-F after the introductory parts; do not infer a complete autonomous Seven Miracles text from this extract.

content_parts.label: 30. The angel and the hermit who judged contrary odors
content_parts.summary: A forty-nine-line angelic-hermit exemplum.

content_parts.label: 31. One judges another for breaking his fast
content_parts.summary: A forty-eight-line warning against harsh judgment.

content_parts.label: 32. St Oswald the king
content_parts.summary: A 142-line royal saint narrative.

content_parts.label: 33. The story of Theophilus
content_parts.summary: A 686-line Theophilus legend concerning apostasy, repentance, and Marian intercession.

content_parts.label: 34. The priest and the maiden
content_parts.summary: A 112-line clerical sexual-sin and repentance exemplum.

content_parts.label: 35. The man who withheld alms to gather money
content_parts.summary: A ninety-eight-line charity and avarice exemplum.

content_parts.label: 36. The holy monk with a wicked brother
content_parts.summary: A 109-line fraternal and monastic salvation exemplum.

content_parts.label: 37. Conversion of Pelagia
content_parts.summary: A 480-line conversion legend of Pelagia.

content_parts.label: 38. The knight who forgave his father's killer
content_parts.summary: An eighty-six-line forgiveness exemplum.

content_parts.label: 39. Gregory prays for a damned man
content_parts.summary: A forty-line Gregory and Trajan intercession narrative. Horstmann places it before item 40, although the Vernon sequence has item 40 first.

content_parts.label: 40. Alexander encloses the Jews within the Caspian mountains
content_parts.summary: A seventy-eight-line Alexander narrative. Its Vernon passage begins before item 39 in manuscript order.

content_parts.label: 41. The three aunts of St Gregory
content_parts.summary: A 118-line narrative of Gregory's three aunts.

content_parts.label: 42. The nun who feigned herself foul
content_parts.summary: A 156-line humility and monastic reputation exemplum.

content_parts.label: 43. The backbiting monk
content_parts.summary: A forty-six-line warning against slander.

content_parts.label: 44. Amon and Mordecai
content_parts.summary: A 238-line Esther narrative, headed in Middle English with Amon and Mordecai.

content_parts.label: 45. Piers the Toller and the white loaf
content_parts.summary: A 350-line charity and salvation narrative. Horstmann reports that Harley 4196 places the parallel at fol. 54 in another homiletic position and notes a variant reading.

content_parts.label: Zusatz: Christ turns water into wine
content_parts.summary: An unnumbered thirty-four-line addendum printed from the middle of the Cana homily after an explanation of water as sin and wine as grace. It is not contiguous with item 45 in the manuscript.
notes: Its separate source locus is why it is mapped independently in the complete Vernon bundle.

related_works.title: Northern Homily Cycle
notes: Most selected narratives are embedded in Gospel homilies of the expanded Vernon recension; the article is not a complete edition of the cycle.

related_works.title: Handlyng Synne
notes: The current Digital Bodleian structure identifies item 29c's source passage at fols. 197r-199r as an extract from Handlyng Synne. This component-level identification does not make Robert Mannyng author of the whole selection.

related_works.title: Barlaam and Josaphat
notes: Horstmann notes the same story as item 28 in Barlaam and Josaphat; exact version dependence is not established.

related_works.title: Legend of St Theodora in the Vernon Manuscript
notes: Horstmann compares its subject matter with item 14. His legacy fol. 92 reference corresponds under the current IIIF structure to fols. 93r-v, canvases 171-172; it is supporting material, not part of the selected item.

evidence.summary: Direct source for the exact title, language attribute, headings, division structure, line counts, printed numbering, notes, and addendum boundary.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon,' Archiv 57 (1877), 241-316.
evidence.summary: Direct source for cataloging-unit boundaries, publication identity, selected contents, editorial responsibilities, page spans, witness comparisons, and item-specific notes.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Vernon Manuscript IIIF manifest, catalog, and item-by-item mapping.
evidence.locator: Lineage evidence evidence:vernon-manifest, evidence:vernon-catalog, and evidence:vernon-mapping
evidence.summary: Supports current shelfmark, witness date and place, physical context, exact non-contiguous folio/canvas mappings, addendum location, and component identification at item 29c.

evidence.citation: John Small, English Metrical Homilies from Manuscripts of the Fourteenth Century (1862).
evidence.summary: Supports Small's editorial role, the earliest Edinburgh witness, supplied comparison witnesses, and the exact page parallels used by Horstmann.

evidence.citation: Anne B. Thompson, ed., The Northern Homily Cycle, Middle English Texts Series (2008), introduction.
evidence.summary: Primary modern scholarly synthesis used for work identity, form, genre, date qualifications, northern localization, recension history, and witness context.

evidence.citation: Deterministic XML structure and extent inspection of CME00062.xml.
evidence.summary: Counted two DIV1, forty-five DIV2, eight DIV3, 111 LG, 7,393 L, three P, seventy-seven PB, 98 NOTE, and 589 italic HI elements; derived individual item line totals and detected the duplicate p. 266 milestone.

open_questions.question: Can the original Northern Homily Cycle's composition date be narrowed beyond around 1300 or the early fourteenth century without adopting a disputed hypothesis as fact?
next_steps: Review post-2008 scholarship on the Carver 1295-1306 proposal and record competing ranges with separate evidence.

open_questions.question: Can an authoritative foliation concordance confirm Horstmann's legacy fol. 126 as current Vernon fol. 167r after the forty-one-leaf lacuna?
next_steps: Consult Vernon codicological literature or ask Bodleian staff; retain both labels in the meantime.

open_questions.question: Was Placidas deliberately printed as a second item 15, or is it an uncorrected error for 16?
next_steps: Look for contemporary errata or later discussion; retain printed 15 and separately expose normalized sequence 16.

open_questions.question: How should item 29's Corpus Christi material, omitted introduction, and selected Handlyng Synne miracles be divided into stable component works?
next_steps: Compare the full Vernon passage with a critical Handlyng Synne edition and the official Bodleian range hierarchy before assigning component authorship or autonomous titles.

open_questions.question: Do all 589 italic spans in the CME encoding represent Horstmann's manuscript-abbreviation expansions, or do some serve another editorial function?
next_steps: Compare queried spans against the mapped complete Vernon facsimile and seek an explicit Horstmann editorial statement; do not mechanically reverse italics into manuscript marks.

notes: The content part list is complete at the printed selection level: forty-five numbered items plus the addendum. Nested sub-exempla inside items 6, 12, and 29 are summarized but not falsely counted as additional top-level numbered items.
The overall form is mixed because the cataloged editorial unit includes prose framing; every medieval content part listed here is verse.
All manuscript page references use current Vernon foliation unless explicitly labeled Horstmann legacy foliation.
The complete Vernon facsimile is locally cached but subject to CC BY-NC 4.0; local availability is not publication permission.
